concepts.title: Idea of the Definite Integration
concepts.conceptBody:
Definite Integration

Definition : 

If $\int f\left( x \right)dx=F\left( x \right)$ i.e. $F\left( x \right)$ be an integral of $f\left( x \right)$, then $F\left( b \right)-F\left( a \right)$ is called the definite integral of $f\left( x \right)$ between the limits $a$ and $b$ and in symbols it is written as $\int _{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx$ or, $\left[ F\left( x \right) \right]$.

Thus if $\int _{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx=F\left( x \right)$ then by definition

$\mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx=[F\left( x \right)]_{a}^{b}=F\left( b \right)-F\left( a \right)$

a is called the lower limit or inferior limit and $b$ is called the upper limit or superior limit.

It is clear that value of a definite integral of a function is unique and it does not depend on different forms of indefinite integral. For if

$\mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx=[F\left( x \right)+c]_{a}^{b}=\left\{ F\left( b \right)+c \right\}=F\left( b \right)-F\left( a \right)$

Thus the value of $\int _{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx$ is same as when we take $\int _{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx=F\left( x \right)$.

concepts.title: Geomerical Significance of the Definite Integration
concepts.conceptBody:
Geometrical Interpretation of Definite Integral 

If $f\left( x \right)>0$ for all $x\in \left[ a,b \right]$; then $\int _{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx$ is numerically equal to the area bounded by the curve $\text{y}=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$, the $\text{x}$-axis and the straight lines $\text{x}=\text{a}$ and $\text{x}=\text{b}$

In general $\int _{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx$ represents algebraic sum of the areas of the figures bounded by the curve $y=f\left( x \right)$, the $x$-axis and the straight lines $x=a$ and $x=b$. The areas above $x$- axis are taken plus sign and the areas below $\text{x}$-axis are taken with minus sign i.e.,

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mathop{ \int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ area }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }APB-\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ area }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }BQC-\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ area }\!\!~\!\!\text{ CRD }\!\!~\!\!\text{ + }\!\!~\!\!\text{ area }\!\!~\!\!\text{ DSTE }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

concepts.title: Changing the Dummy Variable
concepts.conceptBody:
Changing the Dummy Variable 

Property 1: \(\int_a^b f(x) d x=\int_a^b f(t) d t\)

i.e. integration is independent of change of variable.

concepts.title: Interchanging the limits
concepts.conceptBody:
Interchanging the limits 

Property 2: \(\int_a^b f(x) d x=-\int_a^b f(x) d x\)

i.e. if the limits of a definite integral are interchangeable then its value becomes negative of the earlier value.

concepts.title: Splitting the Limits
concepts.conceptBody:
Splitting the Limits

Property 3 : \(\int_a^b f(x) d x=\int_a^c f(x) d x+\int_c^b f(x) d x\)

where $\text{ a}<\text{c}<\text{b}$

Logic : We know that definite integral represents the area of the function between $x-$ axis under the given limits.

Now $\int _{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx=$ area of $f\left( x \right)$ i.e. area ABCEFI

$\begin{array}{l} {} & ~=\text{ }~\text{ area }~\text{ ABCDA }~\text{ }+\text{ }~\text{ area }~\text{ CEFDC }~\text{ } \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{a}^{c}f\left( x \right)dx+\mathop{\int }_{c}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx \\ \end{array}$

You can prove all the above 3 properties by algebraic method. We are leaving that part for you to do it yourself.

General form of Property-3 

$\mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx=\mathop{\int }_{a}^{{{c}_{1}}}f\left( x \right)dx+\mathop{\int }_{{{c}_{1}}}^{{{c}_{2}}}f\left( x \right)dx+\ldots +\mathop{\int }_{{{c}_{n-1}}}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx$

where $\text{ a}<{{\text{c}}_{1}}<{{\text{c}}_{2}}<\ldots <{{\text{c}}_{\text{n}-1}}<\text{b}$

Working Rule 

This property is used when integrand is different in different intervals. This happens in the following cases.

  1. function changes or is discontinuous at some points in [a,b]
  2. Modulus function
  3. Greatest integer function & fractional part.

In each of the 3 cases we find the point where the function is different & divide the interval accordingly using property-3.

Q)

  1. Evaluate $\int _{-1}^{1}f\left( x \right)dx$, where $f\left( x \right)=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1-2x;x\le 0 \\ 1+2x;x\ge 0 \\ \end{array} \right.$ 
  2. Evaluate $\int _{-1}^{1}f\left( x \right)dx$, where $f\left( x \right)=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1-2x;x\le 0 \\ 1+2x;x\ge 0 \\ \end{array} \right.$ 

Solution: 

  1. The function is discontinuous at 0, at its value is changing. Hence we cannot integrate over $\left[ -1,1 \right]$. So applying the rule.

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~\mathop{\int }_{-1}^{1}f\left( x \right)dx=\mathop{\int }_{-1}^{0}f\left( x \right)dx+\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}f\left( x \right)dx \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{-1}^{0}\left( 1-2x \right)dx+\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\left( 1+2x \right)dx \\ {} & ~=\left[ x-{{x}^{2}} \right]_{-1}^{0}+\left[ x+{{x}^{2}} \right]_{0}^{1} \\ {} & ~=\left[ 0-\left( -1-1 \right)\left] + \right[1+1-0 \right]=4 \\ \end{array}$

 

In case of modulus function, the value of function changes at the point where it becomes 0. Hence, breaking the interval

$\begin{array}{l} {} & \text{ }~\text{ Now }~\text{ }\mathop{\int }_{-1}^{1}\left| \text{x} \right|\text{d}x=\mathop{\int }_{-1}^{0}\left| \text{x} \right|\text{dx}+\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\left| \text{x} \right|\text{dx}=\mathop{\int }_{-1}^{0}-\text{xdx}+\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\text{xdx} \\ {} & \begin{array}{l} {} & ~[\because \text{ }~\text{ when }~\text{ }-1<\text{x}<0,\left| \text{x} \right|=-\text{x }~\text{ and }~\text{ when }~\text{ }0<\text{x}<1,\left| \text{x} \right|=\text{x}] \\ {} & ~=-{{\left[ \frac{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{2} \right]}^{0}}+{{\left[ \frac{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{2} \right]}^{1}}=-\left( 0-\frac{1}{2} \right)+\left( \frac{1}{2}-0 \right)=1 \\ \end{array} \\ \end{array}$

3. $\int _{-1}^{3}\left[ \text{x} \right]\text{dx}$ 

 

We know greatest integer function returns integral values only. So for every integral interval value will change.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{-1}^{0}\left[ x \right]dx+\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\left[ x \right]dx+\mathop{\int }_{1}^{2}\left[ x \right]dx+\mathop{\int }_{2}^{3}\left[ x \right]dx \\ {} & ~=-1+0+1+2 \\ {} & ~=2 \\ \end{array}$

4. $\int _{0}^{2}\left\{ x \right\}dx$ 

 

For fractional part, let us draw the graph so for

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 0<x<1 & \left\{ x \right\}=x \\ 1<x<2 & \left\{ x \right\}=x-1 \\ \end{array}$

(this is the reason we did such graphs in functions chapter)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\left\{ x \right\}dx+\mathop{\int }_{1}^{2}\left\{ x \right\}dx=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}xdx+\mathop{\int }_{1}^{2}\left( x-1 \right)dx \\ {} & ~=\left. \frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2} \right|_{0}^{1}+\left. \frac{{{(x-1)}^{2}}}{2} \right|_{1}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1 \\ \end{array}$

 

Q) (a) Find the value of $\int_0^{2 \pi}|\sin x| d x$ (b) Evaluate $\int_0^2\left|\mathrm{x}^2+2 \mathrm{x}-3\right| \mathrm{dx}$ 

Solution : 

  1. [When $\text{sin}x=0,x=n\pi $, where $n=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots $ out of which only $p$ lies between lower and upper limits of integration].

Now $I=\int _{0}^{2\pi }\left| \text{sin}x \right|dx=\int _{0}^{\pi }\left| \text{sin}x \right|dx+\int _{\pi }^{2\pi }\left| \text{sin}x \right|dx$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\text{sin}xdx-\mathop{\int }_{\pi }^{2\pi }\text{sin}xdx=\left[ -\text{cos}x]_{0}^{\pi }- \right[-\text{cos}x]_{\pi }^{2\pi } \\ {} & ~=\left( \text{cos}\pi -\text{cos}0 \right)+\left( \text{cos}2\pi -\text{cos}\pi \right) \\ {} & ~=-\left( -1-1 \right)+\left[ 1-\left( -1 \right) \right]=4 \\ \end{array}$

  1. $x^2+2 x-3=0 \Rightarrow x=-3,1$

Now $I=\int _{0}^{2}\left| {{x}^{2}}+2x-3 \right|dx$

concepts.title: Kings Rule
concepts.conceptBody:
Kings Rule 

Property 4: \(\int_0^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a f(a-x) d x\)

Proof. Put $\text{a}-\text{x}=\text{t}$ on R.H.S.

so lower limit becomes a

and upper limit becomes 0

$\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-\text{dx}=\text{dt}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mathop{ \int }_{0}^{\text{a}}\text{f}\left( \text{a}-\text{x} \right)\text{dx}=\mathop{\int }_{\text{a}}^{0}\text{f}\left( \text{t} \right)\left( -\text{dt} \right)=-\mathop{\int }_{\text{a}}^{0}\text{f}\left( \text{t} \right)\text{dt} & {} \\ =\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{a}}\text{f}\left( \text{t} \right)\text{dt } & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!\{\!\!\text{ using }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Property-2 }\!\!]\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ =\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{a}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx } & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\![\!\!\text{ using }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Property-1 }\!\!]\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array}$

Usefulness 

This property is useful to convert an indefinite integral to a more easily solvable integral. This property is specially very useful in trigonometric integrals. Let us see how.

Illustration 

(a) $\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}}{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}+\sqrt{\text{cos}x}}$ (b) $\int _{0}^{1}x{{(1-x)}^{99}}dx$ 

Solution : 

  1. $I=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}dx}{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}+\sqrt{\text{cos}x}}$ …(i)

see now if you solve this without any use of definite properties, as a normal indefinite question it will become a very lengthy problem.

Let us see how property comes handy in this case.

$I=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}}{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}+\sqrt{\text{cos}x}}dx=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\sqrt{\text{sin}\left( \pi /2-x \right)}}{\sqrt{\text{sin}\left( \pi /2-x \right)}+\sqrt{\text{cos}\left( \pi /2-x \right)}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\sqrt{\text{cos}\left( x \right)}}{\sqrt{\text{cos}x}+\sqrt{\text{sin}x}}$ …(ii)

adding (i) and (ii)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & 2\text{I}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}x}{\sqrt{\text{cos}x}+\sqrt{\text{sin}x}}+\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\sqrt{\text{cos}x}dx}{\sqrt{\text{cos}x}+\sqrt{\text{sin}x}} \\ {} & ~ =\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\left( \frac{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}}{\sqrt{\text{cos}x}+\sqrt{\text{sin}x}}+\frac{\sqrt{\text{cos}x}}{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}+\sqrt{\text{cos}x}} \right)dx \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}+\sqrt{\text{cos}x}}{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}+\sqrt{\text{cos}x}}dx=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}dx \\ {} & ~=\left. \text{x} \right|_{0}^{\pi /2}=\frac{\pi }{2} \\ \therefore & ~\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{I}=\frac{\pi }{2}\Rightarrow \text{I}=\frac{\pi }{4} \\ \end{array}$

So you can notice the usefulness of this property. When $\text{sin}x\And \text{cos}x$ are interchangeable by this property, use it to reduce the integral.

  1. $\int _{0}^{1}x{{(1-x)}^{99}}dx$

Though we can solve this question by first using by parts and then applying the limits, but here we will use this property to prevent that long step.

$\text{I}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\text{x}{{(1-\text{x})}^{99}}\text{dx}$ …(i)

applying Property-4

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~I=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\left( 1-x \right){{[1-\left( 1-x \right)]}^{99}} \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\left( 1-x \right){{x}^{99}}dx \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\left( {{x}^{99}}-{{x}^{100}} \right)dx \\ \end{array}$

Now solve simply as integral of 2 functions (no need of using by parts)

$=\left. \frac{{{x}^{100}}}{100} \right|_{0}^{1}-\left. \frac{{{x}^{101}}}{101} \right|_{0}^{1}=\frac{1}{100}-\frac{1}{101}=\frac{1}{10100}$

Illustration
(a) $\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logtan}xdx$ (b) $\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\text{sin}x-\text{cos}x}{1+\text{sin}x\text{cos}x}dx$ 
(c) $\int _{0}^{\pi /4}\text{log}\left( 1+\text{tanx} \right)\text{dx}$ (d) $\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{x}{\text{sin}x+\text{cos}x}dx$

Solution : 

  1. $\text{I}=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logtanxdx}$ …(i)

applying Property-4

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} (ii) {} & ~I=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logtan}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-2 \right)dx \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logcot}xdx \\ \end{array}$

adding (i) and (ii)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~2I=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logtan}xdx+\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logcot}xdx \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\left( \text{log}\left( \text{tan}x \right)+\text{logcot}x \right)dx \\ \end{array}$

Using the $\text{log}$ property, $\text{log}a+\text{log}b=\text{log}ab$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{log}\left( \text{tanx}\times \text{cotx} \right)\text{dx} \\ {} & =\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{log}1\text{dx}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}0.\text{dx}=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ log}1=0 \right] \\ \end{array}$

Tip. Why I thought of using this property ?

  1. Using by parts, is a very long process
  2. Most importantly, upper limit is $\frac{\pi }{2}$ and every trigonometric, function gives it opposite pair at $\frac{\pi }{2}-\text{x}$, hence purpose solved.
  3. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\text{sin}x-\text{cos}x}{1+\text{sin}x\text{cos}x}dx$ …(1)

Similarly in this question, a bell should ring that upper limit is $\pi /2$ and function comprises

of $\text{sin}x\And \text{cos}x$ which can be interchanged.

Hence applying Property-4.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} …(ii) {} & ~I=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\text{sin}\left( \pi /2-x \right)-\text{cos}\left( \pi /2-x \right)}{1+\text{sin}\left( \pi /2-x \right)\text{cos}\left( \pi /2-x \right)}dx \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\text{cos}x-\text{sin}x}{1+\text{cos}x\text{sin}x}dx \\ \end{array}$

adding (i) and (ii)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~2\text{I}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\left( \frac{\text{sinx}-\text{cosx}}{1+\text{sinxcosx}}+\frac{\text{cosx}-\text{sinx}}{1+\text{sinxcosx}} \right)\text{dx} \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}0.\text{dx}=0 \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & ~\text{I}=0 \\ \end{array}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\int _{0}^{\pi /4}\text{log}\left( 1+\text{tanx} \right)\text{dx}$

applying Property-4

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /4}\text{log}\left( 1+\text{tan}\left( \pi /4-x \right) \right)dx=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /4}\text{log}\left( 1+\frac{\text{tan}\pi /4-\text{tan}x}{1+\text{tan}\pi /4\text{tan}x} \right)dx \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /4}\text{log}\left( 1+\frac{1-\text{tan}x}{1+\text{tan}x} \right)dx ...(ii) \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /4}\text{log}\left( \frac{2}{1+\text{tan}x} \right)dx \\ \end{array}$

adding (i) and (ii)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~\mathop{2I=\int }_{0}^{\pi /4}\left( \text{log}\left( 1+\text{tan}x \right)+\text{log}\left( \frac{2}{1+\text{tan}x} \right) \right)dx \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /4}\text{log}\left( \left( 1+\text{tan}x \right)\frac{2}{\left( 1+\text{tan}x \right)} \right)dx \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /4}\left( \text{log}2 \right)dx \\ \end{array}$

 

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{I}=\text{log}2\int _{0}^{\pi /4}\text{dx}=\left. \text{log}2\text{x} \right|_{0}^{\pi /4}=\frac{\pi }{4}\text{log}2$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\frac{\pi }{8}\text{log}2$

Note. Another common mistake is the last step. Students forget that on L.H.S. it is 2I, and they have to divide by 2 to get the answer. So keep this in mind.

  1. $\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{x}{\text{sin}x+\text{cos}x}dx$

Let $f\left( x \right)=\frac{x}{\text{sin}x+\text{cos}x}$ (i)

Then $f\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right)=\frac{\frac{\pi }{2}-x}{\text{sin}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right)+\text{cos}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right)}$

Or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }f\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right)=\frac{\frac{\pi }{2}-x}{\text{cos}x+\text{sin}x}$

$\left( 1 \right)+\left( 2 \right)\Rightarrow f\left( x \right)+f\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right)=\frac{\pi }{2}\frac{1}{\text{cos}x+\text{sin}x}$

$=\frac{\pi }{2\sqrt{2}\text{cos}\left( x-\frac{\pi }{4} \right)}$

$=\frac{\pi }{2\sqrt{2}}\text{sec}\left( x-\frac{\pi }{4} \right)$

Now $I=\frac{1}{2}\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\left[ f\left( x \right)+f\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right) \right]dx$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{\pi }{2\sqrt{2}}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{sec}\left( \text{x}-\frac{\pi }{4} \right)\text{dx} \\ {} & ~=\frac{\pi }{2\sqrt{2}}\left[ \text{log}\left| \text{sec}\left( \text{x}-\frac{\pi }{4} \right)+\text{tan}\left( \text{x}-\frac{\pi }{4} \right) \right| \right]_{0}^{\pi /2} \\ \end{array}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\frac{\pi }{2\sqrt{2}}\left[ \text{log}\left( \text{cosec}\frac{\pi }{4}+\text{cot}\frac{\pi }{4} \right)-\text{log}\left| \text{sec}\frac{\pi }{4}-\text{tan}\frac{\pi }{4} \right| \right] \\ {} & ~=\frac{\pi }{4\sqrt{2}}\left[ \text{log}\left( \sqrt{2}+1 \right)-\text{log}\left( \sqrt{2}-1 \right) \right] \\ {} & ~=\frac{\pi }{4\sqrt{2}}\text{log}\left( \frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1} \right)=\frac{\pi }{4\sqrt{2}}\text{log}{{(\sqrt{2}+1)}^{2}} \\ {} & ~=\frac{\pi }{2\sqrt{2}}\text{log}\left( \sqrt{2}+1 \right) \\ \end{array}$

Property-5 \(\int_a^b f(x) d x=\int_a^b f(a+b-x) d x\)

Property-4 is a special case of Property-5 when $a=0\And b=a$

Proof. Let $a+b-t=x$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }-\text{dt}=\text{dx } & {} & {} \\ \And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ at }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=\text{a t}=\text{b }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & {} & {} \\ \text{x}=\text{b t}=\text{a} & {} & {} \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\int _{\text{b}}^{\text{a}}\text{f}\left( \text{t} \right)\left( -\text{dt} \right)$

by using Property-2

$I=\mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}f\left( t \right)dt=\mathop{\int }_{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx$

Illustration

(a) $\int _{1}^{2}\frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{3-x}+\sqrt{x}}dx$ (b) $\int _{\pi /6}^{\pi /3}\frac{1}{1+\sqrt{\text{cot}x}}dx$ 

Solution : 

  1. $I=\int _{1}^{2}\frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{3-x}+\sqrt{x}}dx$

If we apply Property-5 in this integral then the denominator remains the same, which gives encouragement to use the same.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ applying Property-5.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~I=\mathop{\int }_{1}^{2}\frac{\sqrt{3-x}}{\sqrt{3-\left( 3-x \right)}+\sqrt{3-x}}d \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{1}^{2}\frac{\sqrt{3-x}dx}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{3-x}} ...(ii) \\ \end{array}$

adding (i) and (ii)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~2I=\mathop{\int }_{1}^{2}\left( \frac{\sqrt{3-x}}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{3-x}}+\frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{3-x}+\sqrt{x}} \right)dx \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{1}^{2}\left( \frac{\sqrt{3-x}+\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{3-x}+\sqrt{x}} \right)dx \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{1}^{2}dx=\left. x \right|_{1}^{2}=2-1=1 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\frac{1}{2}. \\ \end{array}$

  1. $\int _{\pi /6}^{\pi /3}\frac{1}{1+\sqrt{\text{cot}x}}dx=\int _{\pi /6}^{\pi /3}\frac{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}}{\sqrt{\text{sin}x}+\sqrt{\text{cos}x}}dx$

Earlier we have seen that in case of $\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-\text{x} \right)$ transformation in trigonometric integrals, the integral was reduced to a very simple one. See, here also it is happening.

Now, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\pi }{3}+\frac{\pi }{6}=\frac{\pi }{2}$

$\And $ property 5 replaces $\text{x}$ by $\left( \text{a}+\text{b}-\text{x} \right)$ i.e. $\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-\text{x} \right)$

Hence our purpose is solved.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\mathop{\int }_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3}\frac{\sqrt{\text{sin}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right)}}{\sqrt{\text{sin}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right)}+\sqrt{\text{cos}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-x \right)}}dx$

 

$=\mathop{\int }_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3}\frac{\sqrt{\text{cos}x}}{\sqrt{\text{cos}x}+\sqrt{\text{sin}x}}dx$

Now I hope you understand what we are trying to do & what we will do next. adding (i) and (ii)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & 2\text{I}=\mathop{\int }_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3}\text{dx}=\left( \frac{\pi }{3}-\frac{\pi }{6} \right)=\frac{\pi }{6} \\ {} & \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}~=\frac{\pi }{12} \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Odd-Even property
concepts.conceptBody:
Odd-Even property

Property-6 \(\int_{-a}^a~ f(x) d x= \begin{cases}2 \int_0^a~~ f(x) d x, & \text { if } f(x) \text { is even } \\ 0, & \text { if } f(x) \text { is odd }\end{cases}\)

Proof. From Property-3, we get

$\mathop{\int }_{-a}^{a}~~f\left( x \right)dx=\mathop{\int }_{-a}^{0}f\left( x \right)dx+\mathop{\int }_{0}^{a}~~f\left( x \right)dx$ …(i)

Now $\int _{-a}^{0}~~f\left( x \right)dx=\int _{-a}^{0}f\left( -t \right)\left( -dt \right)$ (put $x=-t)$

$\begin{array}{l} {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{a}~~f\left( -t \right)dt=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{a}~~f\left( -x \right)dx \\ {} & ~=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \mathop{\int }_{0}^{a}~~f\left( x \right)dx, & \text{ }~\text{ if }~\text{ }f\left( x \right)\text{ }~\text{ is }~\text{ an }~\text{ even }~\text{ function }~\text{ } \\ -\mathop{\int }_{0}^{a}~~f\left( x \right)dx, & \text{ }~\text{ if }~\text{ }f\left( x \right)\text{ }~\text{ is }~\text{ an }~\text{ odd }~\text{ function }~\text{ } \\ \end{array} \right. \\ \end{array}$ …(ii)

Thus, when $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is an even function from (i) & (ii)

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int _{-a}^{0}~~f\left( x \right)dx=\int _{0}^{a}~~f\left( x \right)dx+\int _{0}^{a}~~f\left( x \right)dx=2\int _{0}^{a}~~f\left( x \right)dx$

and when $f\left( x \right)$ is an odd function, from (i) & (ii)

$\Rightarrow \int _{-a}^{0}~~f\left( x \right)=-\int _{0}^{a}~~f\left( x \right)dx+\int _{0}^{a}~~f\left( x \right)dx=0$

Geometrical Proof. 

If $f(x)$ is EVEN

 

If $f\left( x \right)$ is ODD

 

This property should be used only when limits are equal and opposite and the function which is to be integrated is either odd or even. 

Illustration

(a) Find $\int _{-1}^{1}~~{{x}^{3}}{{e}^{{{x}^{4}}}}dx$ (b) Find $\int _{-1}^{1}~~x\left| x \right|dx$ (c) Evaluate $\int _{-\pi /4}^{\pi /4}~~{{x}^{3}}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{4}}xdx$ 

Solution : 

  1. Let $f\left( x \right)={{x}^{3}}{{e}^{{{x}^{4}}}}$

Then $f\left( -x \right)={{(-x)}^{3}}{{e}^{{{(-x)}^{4}}}}=-{{x}^{3}}{{e}^{{{x}^{4}}}}=-f\left( x \right)$

Hence $f\left( x \right)$ is an odd function.

$\therefore \mathop{\int }_{-1}^{1}~~\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}=0 \text{ or } \text{ }\mathop{\int }_{-1}^{1}~~{{\text{x}}^{3}}{{\text{e}}^{{{\text{x}}^{4}}}}\text{dx}=0$

  1. Let $f\left( x \right)=x\left| x \right|$

$\text{ Then } \text{ }f\left( -x \right)=-x\left| -x \right|=-x\left| x \right|=-f\left( x \right) \left[ \because \left| x \right|=\left| -x \right| \right]$

Hence $f\left( x \right)$ is an odd function.

$\mathop{\int }_{-1}^{1}~~f\left( x \right)dx=0 \text{ or } \mathop{\int }_{-1}^{1}~~\frac{\left| x \right|}{x}dx=0$

  1. Let $f\left( x \right)={{x}^{3}}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{4}}x$

Then $f\left( -x \right)={{(-x)}^{3}}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{4}}\left( -x \right)=-{{x}^{3}}{{(-\text{sin}x)}^{4}}$

$=-{{x}^{3}}\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{4}}x=-f\left( x \right).$

Hence $f\left( x \right)$ is an odd function

$\therefore \mathop{\int }_{-\pi /4}^{\pi /4}~~\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}=0$

concepts.title: Queen Rule
concepts.conceptBody:
Queen Rule

Property-7 $\int_0^{2 a} f(x) d x= \begin{cases}2 \int_0^a f(x) d x ; & \text { if } f(2 a-x)=f(x) \\ 0, & \text { if } f(2 a-x)=-f(x)\end{cases}$

Proof. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int _{0}^{2a}f\left( x \right)dx=\int _{0}^{a}f\left( x \right)dx+\int _{a}^{2a}f\left( x \right)dx$

Put $\text{x}=2\text{a}-\text{t}$ in 2nd integral $\left( \text{dx}=-\text{dt} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ when $\text{x}=\text{a}$ then $\text{t}=\text{a}$

when $x=2a$ then $t=0$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int _{a}^{2a}f\left( x \right)dx=-\int _{a}^{0}f\left( 2a-t \right)dt=\int _{0}^{a}f\left( 2a-t \right)dt=\int _{0}^{a}f\left( 2a-x \right)dx$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int _{0}^{2a}f\left( x \right)dx=\int _{0}^{a}f\left( 2a-x \right)dx$

If $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }f\left( 2a-x \right)=f\left( x \right)$

then $\int _{0}^{2a}f\left( x \right)dx=\int _{0}^{a}f\left( x \right)dx+\int _{0}^{a}f\left( x \right)dx=2\int _{0}^{a}f\left( x \right)dx$

and if $\text{f}\left( 2\text{a}-\text{x} \right)=-\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$

then $\int _{0}^{2a}f\left( x \right)dx=\int _{0}^{a}f\left( x \right)dx-\int _{0}^{a}f\left( x \right)dx=0$

 

Q) Evaluate 

(a) $\int _{0}^{2\pi }\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{5}}xdx$ (b) $\int _{0}^{\pi }\frac{xdx}{1+\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}x}$ 

Solution : 

  1. We will first check for the property-7 conditions. For that let

$f\left( x \right)=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{5}}x$

then $f\left( 2\pi -x \right)=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{5}}\left( 2\pi -x \right)$

$\begin{matrix} =\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{5}}\text{x}=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right) \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mathop{\int }_{0}^{2a}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}=2\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{a}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( 2\text{a}-\text{x} \right)=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right) \\ \end{matrix}$

applying the rule.

$I=2\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{5}}xdx$ …(i)

Now applying property-4

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~I=2\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{5}}\left( \pi -x \right)dx \\ {} & ~=2\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }-\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{5}}xdx \\ {} & ~=-2\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{5}}xdx ...(ii) \\ \end{array}$

adding (i) and (ii)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & 2\text{I}=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{I}=0 \\ \end{array}$

(b) $I=\int _{0}^{\pi }\frac{xdx}{1+\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}x}$

This is an interesting problem, because here. Property- 7 is not visible at first. So the tip that we can derive from this question is that approach the question as given rather than going by a fixed mind.

Here we can see that property-4 is applicable so without thinking anything else I will use it first

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\frac{\left( \pi -x \right)dx}{1+\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\left( \pi -x \right)}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\frac{\left( \pi -x \right)dx}{1+\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}x}$

adding (i) and (ii)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~2I=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\frac{xdx}{1+\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}x}+\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\frac{\pi -x}{1+\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}x}dx \\ {} & =\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\frac{\pi }{1+\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}x}dx \\ \end{array}$

Now if I apply property-4 back then I will have no advantage as I will get the same integral. Hence no use. But if I apply Property-7.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} I=\frac{1}{2}\left[ 2\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\pi \text{dx}}{1+\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\text{x}} \right]\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( 2\text{a}-\text{x} \right)=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right) \\ \begin{align} & \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\pi \text{dx}}{1+\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\text{x}} \\ & \Rightarrow \text{ I}=\frac{1}{2}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\frac{\pi \text{dx}}{1+\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\text{x}} \\ \end{align} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array}$

MISTAKE : Common mistake at this step, is to take it as a normal substitution integral.

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\frac{\pi }{2}\int _{0}^{\pi }\frac{\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}x}{\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}x+1}dx\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ (dividing by $\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}x$ )

Now the common substitution

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & x=\text{tan}x \\ {} & dx=\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}xdx \\ \end{array}$

But wait this is a wrong step, as per the rule of substitution the function which is substituted should be continuous in the interval.

But there for $x=\text{tan}x,\text{tan}x$ is not continuous over interval $\left[ 0,\pi \right]$. It is discontinuous at $x=\frac{\pi }{2}$. Therefore, it is not possible to substitute $\text{tan}x$ in the interval $\left[ 0,\pi \right]$.

So next thought should be to break the interval so that we can apply the transformation.

 

Q) Prove $\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logsin}xdx=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logcos}xdx=-\frac{\pi }{2}\text{log}2$

Solution : Let $I=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logsin}xdx$ …(i)

clearly property- 4 is applicable here,

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{log}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-\text{x} \right)\text{dx}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logcosxdx}$ …(ii)

adding (i) and (ii)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & 2\text{I}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\left( \text{logsinx}+\text{logcosx} \right)\text{dx } \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\left( \text{logsinxcosx} \right)\text{dx}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{log}\left( \frac{\text{sin}2\text{x}}{2} \right)\text{dx } \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\left( \text{logsin}2\text{x}-\text{log}2 \right)\text{dx} \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logsin}2\text{xdx}-\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{log}2\text{dx} \\ {} & ~=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logsin}2\text{xdx}-\text{log}2[\text{x}]_{0}^{\pi /2} \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{I}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logsin}2\text{xdx}-\left( \text{log}2 \right)\left( \frac{\pi }{2} \right) ..(iii) \\ \end{array}$

Now let us solve the integral part separately.

$\text{{I}'}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\left( \text{logsin}2\text{x} \right)\text{dx}$

See if I apply property-4 again here I will again get I', which becomes futile as I am struck here. So what should I do ?

There are only 2 options 

  1. I' gives a definite value, which does not seems to be the case here.
  2. express ${I}'$ in terms of I to solve the question.

Now, let

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 2\text{x}=\text{t} & {} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{dx}=\text{dt} & {} \\ \therefore & \text{{I}'}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\frac{1}{2}\text{log}\left( \text{sint} \right)\text{dt }...\text{(iv)} \\ \end{array}$

Now (iv) is almost similar to I with the only difference being in the upper limit.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ applying property -

$\text{{I}'}=\left( \frac{1}{2} \right)2\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logsintdt }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{log}\left( \text{sint} \right)\text{dt}=\text{I}$

Putting this value back in (iii)

$\begin{matrix} 2\text{I}=\text{I}-\frac{\pi }{2}\text{log}2 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{logsinxdx}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{cosxdx}=-\frac{\pi }{2}\text{log}2 \\ \end{matrix}$

Now solving it as we would have done in indefinite integral case.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} I & ~=\pi \mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}xdx}{1+\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}x} \\ {} & ~=\pi \mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\frac{\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{2}}xdx}{2+\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}x} \\ \end{array}$

Now obviously we will substitute $\text{tanx}=\text{t}$
$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=0 & ~\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ tanx}=\text{t}\to 0 \\ \text{x}=\frac{\pi }{2} & ~\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ tanx}=\text{t}\to \infty \\ \text{I}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\infty }\frac{\pi \text{dt}}{2+{{\text{t}}^{2}}}=\left. \frac{\pi }{\sqrt{2}}\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\frac{\text{t}}{\sqrt{2}} \right|_{0}^{\infty } & ~ \\ =\frac{\pi }{\sqrt{2}}\left( \text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\infty -\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}0 \right)=\frac{\pi }{\sqrt{2}}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-0 \right)=\frac{{{\pi }^{2}}}{2\sqrt{2}} & ~ \\ \end{array}$

Property 8 

Geometrical Proof : 

 

Q) Show that $\int _{0}^{\pi /2}f\left( \text{sin}2x \right)\text{sin}xdx=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}f\left( \text{sin}2x \right)\text{cos}xdx=\sqrt{2}\int _{0}^{\pi /4}f\left( \text{cos}2x \right)\text{cos}xdx$

Solution : 

Let $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}f\left( \text{sin}2x \right)\text{sin}xdx$ …(i)

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\text{f}\left[ \text{sin}2\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-\text{x} \right) \right]\text{sin}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-\text{x} \right)\text{dx}$ (using property-4)

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ I}=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}\text{f}\left[ \text{sin}\left( \pi -2\text{x} \right) \right]\text{cosxdx}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }I=\int _{0}^{\pi /2}f\left( \text{sin}2x \right)\text{cos}xdx$

Hence the first part is proved.

$I=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}f\left( \text{sin}2x \right)\text{sin}xdx$

$=\int _{0}^{\pi /4}\text{f}\left( \text{sin}2\text{x} \right)\text{sinxdx}+\int _{0}^{\pi /4}\text{f}\left[ \text{sin}2\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-\text{x} \right) \right]\text{sin}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}-\text{x} \right)\text{dx }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ (using Property -5$)$

$=\int _{0}^{\pi /4}f\left( \text{sin}2x \right)\text{sin}xdx+\int _{0}^{\pi /4}f\left( \text{sin}2x \right)\text{cos}xdx$

$=\int _{0}^{\pi /4}f\left( \text{sin}2x \right)\left( \text{sin}x+\text{cos}x \right)dx$

$=\int _{0}^{\pi /4}\text{f}\left[ \text{sin}2\left( \frac{\pi }{4}-\text{x} \right) \right]\left[ \text{sin}\left( \frac{\pi }{4}-\text{x} \right)+\text{cosx}\left( \frac{\pi }{4}-\text{x} \right)\text{x} \right]\text{dx }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ (using property-4)

$=\int _{0}^{\pi /4}f\left( \text{cos}2x \right)\left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\text{cos}x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\text{sin}x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\text{cos}x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\text{sin}x \right]dx$

$=\sqrt{2}\int _{0}^{\pi /4}f\left( \text{cos}2x \right)\text{cos}xdx$

Hence the second part is also proved.

concepts.title: Rules based on applying the results of periodic functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Properties related to periodicity

Property-10 : If $f\left( x \right)$ is a periodic function with period $T$ then $\int _{0}^{nT}f\left( x \right)dx=n\int _{0}^{T}f\left( x \right)dx$

The proof of this property is really easy one.

Geometrical Proof. 

If $f\left( x \right)$ is periodic then it will repeat (the curve also) after an interval of $T$.

Since the area under the curve for an interval of $\text{T}$ is same every time.

Total area $=\text{n}\times ($ curve under one interval $)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int _{0}^{\text{nT}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}\setminus =\text{n}\int _{0}^{\text{T}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}$

Property-11 \(\int_a^{a+T} f(x) d x=\int_0^T f(x) d x\)

Let us see the proof using geometry

Now

so observation only we can say

$\mathop{\int }_{0}^{T}f\left( x \right)dx=\mathop{\int }_{a}^{a+T}f\left( x \right)dx$

Property-12 : Generalization of the above property is

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \\ \mathop{\int }_{a}^{a+nT}f\left( x \right)dx=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{nT}f\left( x \right)dx=n\mathop{\int }_{0}^{T}f\left( x \right)dx \\ \end{array}$

We can also use geometrical proof here.

Property-13: $ n,m\in I$

Geometrical Proof. 

We can write,

$\mathop{\int }_{\text{mT}}^{\text{nT}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{nT}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}-\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{mT}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}$

i.e. area of curve between $=$ area of curve uptil $\text{nT}$ - area of curve uptil $\text{mT}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\mathop{ \int }_{\text{mT}}^{\text{nT}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}~=\text{n}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{T}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}-\text{m}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{T}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx} & {} \\ =\left( \text{n}-\text{m} \right)\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{T}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx } & ~ \\ \end{array}$

Some other properties deduced from earlier properties 

  1. $\int _{n\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}^{\text{a}+\text{nT}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}=\int _{0}^{\text{a}}\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{dx}$
  2. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int _{a+mT}^{a+nT}f\left( x \right)dx=\int _{mT}^{nT}f\left( x \right)dx$
  3. $\int _{a+nT}^{b+nT}f\left( x \right)dx=\int _{a}^{b}f\left( x \right)dx$

 

Q) (a) Prove that $\int _{0}^{10}\left( x-\left[ x \right] \right)dx=5$. (b) $\int _{0}^{100}{{e}^{x-\left[ x \right]}}dx=100\left( e-1 \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$(c)$\int _{0}^{400\pi }\sqrt{1-\text{cos}2x}dx=800\sqrt{2}$ 

Solution : 

(a) Since $\text{x}-\left[ \text{x} \right]$ is a periodic function with period one unit. Therefore

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \mathop{\int }_{0}^{10}\left( x-\left[ x \right] \right)dx & ~=10\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\left( x-\left[ x \right] \right)dx=10\left[ \mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}xdx-\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}\left[ x \right]dx \right] \\ {} & ~=10\left[ \left[ \frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2} \right]_{0}^{1}-0 \right]=\frac{10}{2}=5 \\ \end{array}$

(b) Since $x-\left[ x \right]$ is a periodic function with period one unit, therefore so is ${{e}^{x-\left[ x \right]}}$, and hence

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~\mathop{\int }_{0}^{100}{{e}^{x}}-\left[ x \right]dx=100\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}{{e}^{x-\left[ x \right]}}dx=100\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}{{e}^{x-0}}dx \\ {} & ~=100\mathop{\int }_{0}^{1}{{e}^{x}}\cdot dx=100\left( e-1 \right) \\ \end{array}$

(c) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int _{0}^{400\pi }\sqrt{1-\text{cos}2x}=\int _{0}^{400\pi }\sqrt{2}\left| \text{sin}x \right|dx$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\sqrt{2}\times 400\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\left| \text{sin}x \right|dx\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \because \left| \text{sin}x \right|\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ periodic }\!\!~\!\!\text{ with }\!\!~\!\!\text{ period }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\pi \right] \\ {} & ~=400\sqrt{2}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\text{sin}xdx=400\sqrt{2}[-\text{cos}x]_{0}^{\pi }=800\sqrt{2} \\ \end{array}$

 

Q) (a) Evaluate $\int _{0}^{4\pi }\left| \text{cos}x \right|dx$ (b) Evaluate $\int _{0}^{32\pi /3}\sqrt{1+\text{cos}2x}dx$ 

Solution : 

  1. Note that $\left| \text{cos}x \right|$ is a periodic with period $\pi $.

Hence the given integral,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~\text{I}=4\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\left| \text{cos}x \right|dx \\ {} & ~=4\left\{ \mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{cos}xdx-\mathop{\int }_{\pi /2}^{\pi }\text{cos}xdx \right\} \\ {} & ~=4\left\{ (\text{sin}x)_{0}^{\pi /2}-(\text{sin}x)_{\pi /2}^{\pi } \right\}=4\left( 1+1 \right)=8 \\ \end{array}$

  1. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int _{0}^{32\pi /3}\sqrt{1+\text{cos}2x}dx=\sqrt{2}\int _{0}^{10\pi }\left| \text{cos}x \right|dx+\sqrt{2}\int _{10\pi }^{32\pi /3}\left| \text{cos}x \right|dx$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=10\sqrt{2}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\left| \text{cos}x \right|dx+\sqrt{2}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{2\pi /3}\left| \text{cos}x \right|dx \\ {} & ~=10\sqrt{2}\left[ \mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{cos}xdx-\mathop{\int }_{\pi /2}^{\pi }\text{cos}xdx \right]+\sqrt{2}\left[ \mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{cos}xdx+\mathop{\int }_{\pi /2}^{2\pi /3}-\text{cos}xdx \right] \\ {} & ~=10\sqrt{2}\left[ 1+1 \right]+\sqrt{2}\left[ 1-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+1 \right] \\ {} & ~=20\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{2}\left( 2-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)=22\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} \\ \end{array}$

 

Q) Show $\int _{0}^{n\pi +V}\left| \text{sin}x \right|dx=\left( 2n+1 \right)-\text{cos}V$, where $n$ is positive integer. and $0\le V<\pi $. 

Solution : 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{n}\pi +\text{V}}\left| \text{sinx} \right|\text{dx}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{V}}\left| \text{sinx} \right|\text{dx}+\mathop{\int }_{\text{V}}^{\text{n}\pi +\text{V}}\left| \text{sinx} \right|\text{dx } & ~ \\ =\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{V}}\text{sinxdx}+\text{n}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\left| \text{sinx} \right|\text{dx }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (Using }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Property-IX)} & ~\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ =(-\text{cosx})_{0}^{\text{V}}+\text{n}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi }\text{sinxdx } & ~ \\ =\left( -\text{cosV}+1 \right)+\text{n}(-\text{cosx})_{0}^{\pi } & ~ \\ ~=-\left( \text{cosV} \right)+1+\text{n}\left( 1+1 \right) & {} \\ ~=\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)-\text{cosV } & {} \\ \Rightarrow ~\mathop{ \int }_{0}^{\text{n}\pi +\text{V}}\left| \text{sinx} \right|\text{dx}=\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)-\text{cosV }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & {} \\ \end{array}$

where $\text{n}$ is positive integer and $0\le \text{V}<\pi $.

concepts.title: Mean Value Theorem of integration
concepts.conceptBody:
Remaining properties

Property-21 : If $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a continuous function on $\left[ \text{a},\text{b} \right]$ then there exists a point $\text{c}\in \left( \text{a},\text{b} \right)$ such that $\int _{a}^{b}~~f\left( x \right)dx=f\left( c \right)\left( b-a \right)$. This is known as Mean Value Theorem of Integration.

Property-22 : If $f\left( x \right)$ is continuous in $\left[ a,b \right]~\And~ \int _{a}^{b}~~f\left( x \right)=0$ then the equation $f\left( x \right)=0$ has atleast one root in $\left( \text{a},\text{b} \right)$.
